does: This paper proposes a training-free jailbreak detection method that uses semantic similarity between multilingual query embeddings and a fixed English codebook of known jailbreak prompts to detect adversarial attacks across languages. The authors evaluate their approach systematically across four languages, multiple translation methods, safety benchmarks, embedding models, and LLMs including Qwen, Llama, and GPT-3.5, finding that semantic similarity generalizes well on curated benchmarks with canonical jailbreak templates, achieving near-perfect separability with AUC up to 0.99, while revealing performance degradation on more naturalistic, diverse attacks.
connects: This work directly addresses the cross-lingual vulnerability of safety mechanisms identified in prior research showing that translating malicious prompts into non-English languages significantly increases jailbreak success rates. It builds on established approaches in adversarial defense and representation learning by leveraging multilingual embeddings to achieve language-agnostic threat detection. The paper extends prior work on semantic similarity-based safety mechanisms by demonstrating that English-centric jailbreak codebooks can serve as universal references across languages, while also engaging with broader research on the robustness of safety training and the structural gaps in multilingual LLM deployment.
enables: This approach opens pathways for improving cross-lingual safety in LLMs without language-specific retraining, positioning semantic codebooks as practical guardrails for black-box model deployment. The work points toward future research on bridging the gap between curated and naturalistic attack scenarios, developing more robust multilingual threat modeling, and exploring whether adversarial examples can be crafted to evade semantic similarity defenses. It also enables investigation into whether combining semantic detection with other safety mechanisms could achieve more reliable protection, and raises questions about the limits of translation-invariant attack detection in increasingly sophisticated adversarial settings.
